The mountain’s forests of [[:en:Castanopsis|Castanopsis]] and {{ill|Quercus|ja|カシ|qq=|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Quercus|qq=}} trees contribute to its scenic beauty, making it a popular spot on the {{ill|Kinki Nature Trail|ja|近畿自然歩道|qq=|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinki Nature Trail|qq=}}. While the advent of GPS has reduced its significance as a navigational aid, the area is preserved as a {{ill|Protective Forest for Navigation|ja|航行目標保安林|qq=|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Protective Forest for Navigation|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. The summit features an observation deck and a digital television relay station, the {{ill|Isobe Relay Station|ja|磯部中継局|qq=|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Isobe Relay Station|qq=}}. The haiku pen name of {{ill|Seihō Shimada|ja|嶋田青峰|qq=|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seihō Shimada|qq=}}, a poet from nearby Matoya, is derived from this mountain.<ref>Hosoi (1996): p.48</ref> On May 25, 2016, Canadian Prime Minister {{ill|Justin Trudeau|en|qq=|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Justin Trudeau|qq=}} and his wife {{ill|Sophie Grégoire Trudeau|en|qq=|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sophie Grégoire Trudeau|qq=}} hiked Mount Aonomine during their visit for the {{ill|42nd G7 Summit|en|第42回先進国首脳会議|qq=|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/42nd G7 Summit|qq=}} in Ise-Shima.<ref>"Canadian Prime Minister Enjoys Hiking with His Wife on Their Wedding Anniversary," *Chunichi Shimbun*, May 26, 2016</ref> == Geography and Geology == Mount Aonomine is classified as part of the "Eastern Terrace District," a 100–115 meter hilly region within Toba City.<ref>Toba City History Compilation Office (1991): Upper Volume, p.18</ref> It serves as the source of the {{ill|Kamo River (Mie Prefecture)|ja|加茂川|qq=|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamo River (Mie Prefecture)|qq=}}, a secondary river.<ref>Toba City History Compilation Office (1991): Upper Volume, p.20</ref> Geologically, it belongs to the {{ill|Matoya Formation|ja|的矢層群|qq=|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Matoya Formation|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, which is part of the {{ill|Shimanto Belt|ja|四万十層群|qq=|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shimanto Belt|qq=}}, formed during the [[:en:Mesozoic|Mesozoic Era]].<ref>Kawai (1967): p.44</ref> == Aonomine Trails == The mountain has several trails collectively known as "Aonomine Trails": * '''Matsuo Trail''': Two routes (old and new) starting from Matsuo in Toba City. * '''Kenko Trail''': A route starting in Kenko in Toba City. * '''Isobe Trail''': Starting from Izawa-no-Miya Shrine in Shima City. * '''Matoya Trail''': A drivable route from Matoya Shrine in Shima City. * '''Gochi Trail''': A pedestrian-only route starting near [[:en:Gochi Station|Gochi Station]] in Shima City. == References == {{reflist|qq=}} == Further Reading == * Isobe Town History Compilation Committee, *Isobe Town History: Upper Volume*, 1997. * Kawai Masatora, "Exploring the Origins of the Japanese Archipelago," *Geological News*, 1967. * Toba City History Compilation Office, *Toba City History: Upper and Lower Volumes*, 1991. * Hosoi Keiji, "Seihō Shimada and the Haiku Literary Movement," *Haiku Literature Quarterly*, 1996. * Mie Prefecture Board of Education, *Historical Pathways of Ise: Asama-Toba Road and Others*, 1986. == Related Articles == * [[:en:Ise-Shima National Park|Ise-Shima National Park]] * [[:en:Sea lane|Navigation]] * [[:en:Sea mark|Landmarks]] * [[:en:Hiking|Hiking]] * {{ill|Hiyoriyama (Toba City)|ja|日和山 (鳥羽市)|qq=|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hiyoriyama (Toba City)|qq=}} == External Links == * [http://maikoh.net/corp/event/aominezan/ Shofuku-ji Temple - Aonomine Mountain Official Page]

It has an elevation of 336 meters. == Overview == Despite its relatively low elevation, Mount Aonomine offers clear views of [[:en:Ise Bay|Ise Bay]], [[:en:Enshū Sea|Enshū-nada Sea]], and [[:en:Kumano Sea|Kumano-nada Sea]], making it a landmark for {{ill|kaisen|ja|廻船|qq=|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kaisen|qq=}} sailors in ancient times. their visit for the {{ill|42nd G7 Summit|en|第42回先進国首脳会議|qq=|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/42nd G7 Summit|qq=}} in Ise-Shima.<ref>"Canadian Prime Minister Enjoys Hiking with His Wife on Their Wedding Anniversary," *Chunichi Shimbun*, May 26, 2016</ref> == Geography and Geology == Mount Aonomine is classified as part of the "Eastern Terrace District," a 100–115 meter hilly region within Toba City.<ref>Toba City History Compilation Office (1991): Upper Volume, p.18</ref> It serves as the source of the {{ill|Kamo River (Mie Prefecture)|ja|加茂川|qq=|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamo River (Mie Prefecture)|qq=}}, a secondary river.<ref>Toba City History Compilation Office (1991): Upper Volume, p.20</ref> Geologically, it belongs to the {{ill|Matoya Formation|ja|的矢層群|qq=|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Matoya Formation|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, which is part of the {{ill|Shimanto Belt|ja|四万十層群|qq=|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shimanto Belt|qq=}}, formed during the [[:en:Mesozoic|Mesozoic Era]].<ref>Kawai (1967): p.44</ref> == Aonomine Trails == The mountain has several trails collectively known as "Aonomine Trails": * '''Matsuo Trail''': Two routes (old and new) starting from Matsuo in Toba City. * '''Kenko Trail''': A route starting in Kenko in Toba City. * '''Isobe Trail''': Starting from Izawa-no-Miya Shrine in Shima City. * '''Matoya Trail''': A drivable route from Matoya Shrine in Shima City. * '''Gochi Trail''': A pedestrian-only route starting near [[:en:Gochi Station|Gochi Station]] in Shima City. == References == {{reflist|qq=}} == Further Reading == * Isobe Town History Compilation Committee, *Isobe Town History: Upper Volume*, 1997. * Kawai Masatora, "Exploring the Origins of the Japanese Archipelago," *Geological News*, 1967. * Toba City History Compilation Office, *Toba City History: Upper and Lower Volumes*, 1991. * Hosoi Keiji, "Seihō Shimada and the Haiku Literary Movement," *Haiku Literature Quarterly*, 1996. * Mie Prefecture Board of Education, *Historical Pathways of Ise: Asama-Toba Road and Others*, 1986. == Related Articles == * [[:en:Ise-Shima National Park|Ise-Shima National Park]] * [[:en:Sea lane|Navigation]] * [[:en:Sea mark|Landmarks]] * [[:en:Hiking|Hiking]] * {{ill|Hiyoriyama (Toba City)|ja|日和山 (鳥羽市)|qq=|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hiyoriyama (Toba City)|qq=}} == External Links == * [http://maikoh.net/corp/event/aominezan/ Shofuku-ji Temple - Aonomine Mountain Official Page]

revision.comment: Bot: remove deleted category Sacred Mountains of Japan
revision.text:
{{Infobox mountain | name = Mount Aonomine | image = [[File:Mt. Aonomine from Shimonogo, Isobe.jpg|250px]] | caption = View from the Shima City side | elevation_m = 336.3 | coordinates = {{coord|34|24|32|N|136|49|27|E|type:mountain|display=inline,title|qq=}} | location = {{JPN|qq=}} [[Mie Prefecture]] ([[Toba City]] and [[Shima City]]) | range = Isolated mountain | type = Low-altitude, solitary mountain<ref>Toba City History Compilation Office (1991): Upper Volume, p.23</ref> }} '''Mount Aonomine''' (青峰山, Aonomine-san<ref>Mie Prefecture (2005) "[http://www.pref.mie.jp/KAWASABO/HP/kasen/seibi/kamokihon.pdf Basic Policy for River Improvement in the Kamo River System]" p.3</ref>) is a mountain located on the border between [[:en:Toba, Mie|Toba City]] and [[:en:Shima, Mie|Shima City]] in [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]]. It has an elevation of 336 meters. == Overview == Despite its relatively low elevation, Mount Aonomine offers clear views of [[:en:Ise Bay|Ise Bay]], [[:en:Enshū Sea|Enshū-nada Sea]], and [[:en:Kumano Sea|Kumano-nada Sea]], making it a landmark for {{ill|kaisen|ja|廻船|qq=|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kaisen|qq=}} sailors in ancient times. The mountain is home to {{ill|Shofuku-ji Temple|ja|正福寺 (鳥羽市)|qq=|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shofuku-ji Temple|qq=}}, which enshrines a golden statue of {{ill|Kannon|ja|観音|qq=|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kannon|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 観音菩薩}} riding on a {{ill|whale|ja|鯨|qq=|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/whale|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to クジラ}}. The temple has historically been a site for maritime safety prayers. The mountain’s forests of [[:en:Castanopsis|Castanopsis]] and {{ill|Quercus|ja|カシ|qq=|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Quercus|qq=}} trees contribute to its scenic beauty, making it a popular spot on the {{ill|Kinki Nature Trail|ja|近畿自然歩道|qq=|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinki Nature Trail|qq=}}. While the advent of GPS has reduced its significance as a navigational aid, the area is preserved as a {{ill|Protective Forest for Navigation|ja|航行目標保安林|qq=|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Protective Forest for Navigation|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. The summit features an observation deck and a digital television relay station, the {{ill|Isobe Relay Station|ja|磯部中継局|qq=|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Isobe Relay Station|qq=}}. The haiku pen name of {{ill|Seihō Shimada|ja|嶋田青峰|qq=|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seihō Shimada|qq=}}, a poet from nearby Matoya, is derived from this mountain.<ref>Hosoi (1996): p.48</ref> On May 25, 2016, Canadian Prime Minister {{ill|Justin Trudeau|en|qq=|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Justin Trudeau|qq=}} and his wife {{ill|Sophie Grégoire Trudeau|en|qq=|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sophie Grégoire Trudeau|qq=}} hiked Mount Aonomine during their visit for the {{ill|42nd G7 Summit|en|第42回先進国首脳会議|qq=|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/42nd G7 Summit|qq=}} in Ise-Shima.<ref>"Canadian Prime Minister Enjoys Hiking with His Wife on Their Wedding Anniversary," *Chunichi Shimbun*, May 26, 2016</ref> == Geography and Geology == Mount Aonomine is classified as part of the "Eastern Terrace District," a 100–115 meter hilly region within Toba City.<ref>Toba City History Compilation Office (1991): Upper Volume, p.18</ref> It serves as the source of the {{ill|Kamo River (Mie Prefecture)|ja|加茂川|qq=|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamo River (Mie Prefecture)|qq=}}, a secondary river.<ref>Toba City History Compilation Office (1991): Upper Volume, p.20</ref> Geologically, it belongs to the {{ill|Matoya Formation|ja|的矢層群|qq=|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Matoya Formation|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, which is part of the {{ill|Shimanto Belt|ja|四万十層群|qq=|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shimanto Belt|qq=}}, formed during the [[:en:Mesozoic|Mesozoic Era]].<ref>Kawai (1967): p.44</ref> == Aonomine Trails == The mountain has several trails collectively known as "Aonomine Trails": * '''Matsuo Trail''': Two routes (old and new) starting from Matsuo in Toba City. * '''Kenko Trail''': A route starting in Kenko in Toba City. * '''Isobe Trail''': Starting from Izawa-no-Miya Shrine in Shima City. * '''Matoya Trail''': A drivable route from Matoya Shrine in Shima City. * '''Gochi Trail''': A pedestrian-only route starting near [[:en:Gochi Station|Gochi Station]] in Shima City. == References == {{reflist|qq=}} == Further Reading == * Isobe Town History Compilation Committee, *Isobe Town History: Upper Volume*, 1997. * Kawai Masatora, "Exploring the Origins of the Japanese Archipelago," *Geological News*, 1967. * Toba City History Compilation Office, *Toba City History: Upper and Lower Volumes*, 1991. * Hosoi Keiji, "Seihō Shimada and the Haiku Literary Movement," *Haiku Literature Quarterly*, 1996. * Mie Prefecture Board of Education, *Historical Pathways of Ise: Asama-Toba Road and Others*, 1986. == Related Articles == * [[:en:Ise-Shima National Park|Ise-Shima National Park]] * [[:en:Sea lane|Navigation]] * [[:en:Sea mark|Landmarks]] * [[:en:Hiking|Hiking]] * {{ill|Hiyoriyama (Toba City)|ja|日和山 (鳥羽市)|qq=|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hiyoriyama (Toba City)|qq=}} == External Links == * [http://maikoh.net/corp/event/aominezan/ Shofuku-ji Temple - Aonomine Mountain Official Page]

revision.comment: Bot: convert local link → enwiki interwiki
revision.text:
{{Infobox mountain | name = Mount Aonomine | image = [[File:Mt. Aonomine from Shimonogo, Isobe.jpg|250px]] | caption = View from the Shima City side | elevation_m = 336.3 | coordinates = {{coord|34|24|32|N|136|49|27|E|type:mountain|display=inline,title|qq=}} | location = {{JPN|qq=}} [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]] ([[Toba City]] and [[:en:Shima City|Shima City]]) | range = Isolated mountain | type = Low-altitude, solitary mountain<ref>Toba City History Compilation Office (1991): Upper Volume, p.23</ref> }} '''Mount Aonomine''' (青峰山, Aonomine-san<ref>Mie Prefecture (2005) "[http://www.pref.mie.jp/KAWASABO/HP/kasen/seibi/kamokihon.pdf Basic Policy for River Improvement in the Kamo River System]" p.3</ref>) is a mountain located on the border between [[:en:Toba, Mie|Toba City]] and [[:en:Shima, Mie|Shima City]] in [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]]. It has an elevation of 336 meters. == Overview == Despite its relatively low elevation, Mount Aonomine offers clear views of [[:en:Ise Bay|Ise Bay]], [[:en:Enshū Sea|Enshū-nada Sea]], and [[:en:Kumano Sea|Kumano-nada Sea]], making it a landmark for {{ill|kaisen|ja|廻船|qq=|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kaisen|qq=}} sailors in ancient times. The mountain is home to {{ill|Shofuku-ji Temple|ja|正福寺 (鳥羽市)|qq=|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shofuku-ji Temple|qq=}}, which enshrines a golden statue of {{ill|Kannon|ja|観音|qq=|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kannon|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 観音菩薩}} riding on a {{ill|whale|ja|鯨|qq=|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/whale|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to クジラ}}. The temple has historically been a site for maritime safety prayers. The mountain’s forests of [[:en:Castanopsis|Castanopsis]] and {{ill|Quercus|ja|カシ|qq=|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Quercus|qq=}} trees contribute to its scenic beauty, making it a popular spot on the {{ill|Kinki Nature Trail|ja|近畿自然歩道|qq=|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinki Nature Trail|qq=}}. While the advent of GPS has reduced its significance as a navigational aid, the area is preserved as a {{ill|Protective Forest for Navigation|ja|航行目標保安林|qq=|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Protective Forest for Navigation|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. The summit features an observation deck and a digital television relay station, the {{ill|Isobe Relay Station|ja|磯部中継局|qq=|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Isobe Relay Station|qq=}}. The haiku pen name of {{ill|Seihō Shimada|ja|嶋田青峰|qq=|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seihō Shimada|qq=}}, a poet from nearby Matoya, is derived from this mountain.<ref>Hosoi (1996): p.48</ref> On May 25, 2016, Canadian Prime Minister {{ill|Justin Trudeau|en|qq=|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Justin Trudeau|qq=}} and his wife {{ill|Sophie Grégoire Trudeau|en|qq=|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sophie Grégoire Trudeau|qq=}} hiked Mount Aonomine during their visit for the {{ill|42nd G7 Summit|en|第42回先進国首脳会議|qq=|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/42nd G7 Summit|qq=}} in Ise-Shima.<ref>"Canadian Prime Minister Enjoys Hiking with His Wife on Their Wedding Anniversary," *Chunichi Shimbun*, May 26, 2016</ref> == Geography and Geology == Mount Aonomine is classified as part of the "Eastern Terrace District," a 100–115 meter hilly region within Toba City.<ref>Toba City History Compilation Office (1991): Upper Volume, p.18</ref> It serves as the source of the {{ill|Kamo River (Mie Prefecture)|ja|加茂川|qq=|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamo River (Mie Prefecture)|qq=}}, a secondary river.<ref>Toba City History Compilation Office (1991): Upper Volume, p.20</ref> Geologically, it belongs to the {{ill|Matoya Formation|ja|的矢層群|qq=|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Matoya Formation|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, which is part of the {{ill|Shimanto Belt|ja|四万十層群|qq=|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shimanto Belt|qq=}}, formed during the [[:en:Mesozoic|Mesozoic Era]].<ref>Kawai (1967): p.44</ref> == Aonomine Trails == The mountain has several trails collectively known as "Aonomine Trails": * '''Matsuo Trail''': Two routes (old and new) starting from Matsuo in Toba City. * '''Kenko Trail''': A route starting in Kenko in Toba City. * '''Isobe Trail''': Starting from Izawa-no-Miya Shrine in Shima City. * '''Matoya Trail''': A drivable route from Matoya Shrine in Shima City. * '''Gochi Trail''': A pedestrian-only route starting near [[:en:Gochi Station|Gochi Station]] in Shima City. == References == {{reflist|qq=}} == Further Reading == * Isobe Town History Compilation Committee, *Isobe Town History: Upper Volume*, 1997. * Kawai Masatora, "Exploring the Origins of the Japanese Archipelago," *Geological News*, 1967. * Toba City History Compilation Office, *Toba City History: Upper and Lower Volumes*, 1991. * Hosoi Keiji, "Seihō Shimada and the Haiku Literary Movement," *Haiku Literature Quarterly*, 1996. * Mie Prefecture Board of Education, *Historical Pathways of Ise: Asama-Toba Road and Others*, 1986. == Related Articles == * [[:en:Ise-Shima National Park|Ise-Shima National Park]] * [[:en:Sea lane|Navigation]] * [[:en:Sea mark|Landmarks]] * [[:en:Hiking|Hiking]] * {{ill|Hiyoriyama (Toba City)|ja|日和山 (鳥羽市)|qq=|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hiyoriyama (Toba City)|qq=}} == External Links == * [http://maikoh.net/corp/event/aominezan/ Shofuku-ji Temple - Aonomine Mountain Official Page]

{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11661367]] Wikidata name: Mount Aonomine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 02:35, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox mountain | name = Mount Aonomine | image = [[File:Mt. Aonomine from Shimonogo, Isobe.jpg|250px]] | caption = View from the Shima City side | elevation_m = 336.3 | coordinates = {{coord|34|24|32|N|136|49|27|E|type:mountain|display=inline,title|qq=}} | location = {{JPN|qq=}} [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]] ([[:en:Toba City|Toba City]] and [[:en:Shima City|Shima City]]) | range = Isolated mountain | type = Low-altitude, solitary mountain<ref>Toba City History Compilation Office (1991): Upper Volume, p.23</ref> }} '''Mount Aonomine''' (青峰山, Aonomine-san<ref>Mie Prefecture (2005) "[http://www.pref.mie.jp/KAWASABO/HP/kasen/seibi/kamokihon.pdf Basic Policy for River Improvement in the Kamo River System]" p.3</ref>) is a mountain located on the border between [[:en:Toba, Mie|Toba City]] and [[:en:Shima, Mie|Shima City]] in [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]]. It has an elevation of 336 meters. == Overview == Despite its relatively low elevation, Mount Aonomine offers clear views of [[:en:Ise Bay|Ise Bay]], [[:en:Enshū Sea|Enshū-nada Sea]], and [[:en:Kumano Sea|Kumano-nada Sea]], making it a landmark for {{ill|kaisen|ja|廻船|qq=|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kaisen|qq=}} sailors in ancient times. The mountain is home to {{ill|Shofuku-ji Temple|ja|正福寺 (鳥羽市)|qq=|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shofuku-ji Temple|qq=}}, which enshrines a golden statue of {{ill|Kannon|ja|観音|qq=|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kannon|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 観音菩薩}} riding on a {{ill|whale|ja|鯨|qq=|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/whale|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to クジラ}}. The temple has historically been a site for maritime safety prayers. The mountain’s forests of [[:en:Castanopsis|Castanopsis]] and {{ill|Quercus|ja|カシ|qq=|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Quercus|qq=}} trees contribute to its scenic beauty, making it a popular spot on the {{ill|Kinki Nature Trail|ja|近畿自然歩道|qq=|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinki Nature Trail|qq=}}. While the advent of GPS has reduced its significance as a navigational aid, the area is preserved as a {{ill|Protective Forest for Navigation|ja|航行目標保安林|qq=|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Protective Forest for Navigation|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. The summit features an observation deck and a digital television relay station, the {{ill|Isobe Relay Station|ja|磯部中継局|qq=|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Isobe Relay Station|qq=}}. The haiku pen name of {{ill|Seihō Shimada|ja|嶋田青峰|qq=|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seihō Shimada|qq=}}, a poet from nearby Matoya, is derived from this mountain.<ref>Hosoi (1996): p.48</ref> On May 25, 2016, Canadian Prime Minister {{ill|Justin Trudeau|en|qq=|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Justin Trudeau|qq=}} and his wife {{ill|Sophie Grégoire Trudeau|en|qq=|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sophie Grégoire Trudeau|qq=}} hiked Mount Aonomine during their visit for the {{ill|42nd G7 Summit|en|第42回先進国首脳会議|qq=|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/42nd G7 Summit|qq=}} in Ise-Shima.<ref>"Canadian Prime Minister Enjoys Hiking with His Wife on Their Wedding Anniversary," *Chunichi Shimbun*, May 26, 2016</ref> == Geography and Geology == Mount Aonomine is classified as part of the "Eastern Terrace District," a 100–115 meter hilly region within Toba City.<ref>Toba City History Compilation Office (1991): Upper Volume, p.18</ref> It serves as the source of the {{ill|Kamo River (Mie Prefecture)|ja|加茂川|qq=|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamo River (Mie Prefecture)|qq=}}, a secondary river.<ref>Toba City History Compilation Office (1991): Upper Volume, p.20</ref> Geologically, it belongs to the {{ill|Matoya Formation|ja|的矢層群|qq=|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Matoya Formation|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, which is part of the {{ill|Shimanto Belt|ja|四万十層群|qq=|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shimanto Belt|qq=}}, formed during the [[:en:Mesozoic|Mesozoic Era]].<ref>Kawai (1967): p.44</ref> == Aonomine Trails == The mountain has several trails collectively known as "Aonomine Trails": * '''Matsuo Trail''': Two routes (old and new) starting from Matsuo in Toba City. * '''Kenko Trail''': A route starting in Kenko in Toba City. * '''Isobe Trail''': Starting from Izawa-no-Miya Shrine in Shima City. * '''Matoya Trail''': A drivable route from Matoya Shrine in Shima City. * '''Gochi Trail''': A pedestrian-only route starting near [[:en:Gochi Station|Gochi Station]] in Shima City. == References == {{reflist|qq=}} == Further Reading == * Isobe Town History Compilation Committee, *Isobe Town History: Upper Volume*, 1997. * Kawai Masatora, "Exploring the Origins of the Japanese Archipelago," *Geological News*, 1967. * Toba City History Compilation Office, *Toba City History: Upper and Lower Volumes*, 1991. * Hosoi Keiji, "Seihō Shimada and the Haiku Literary Movement," *Haiku Literature Quarterly*, 1996. * Mie Prefecture Board of Education, *Historical Pathways of Ise: Asama-Toba Road and Others*, 1986. == Related Articles == * [[:en:Ise-Shima National Park|Ise-Shima National Park]] * [[:en:Sea lane|Navigation]] * [[:en:Sea mark|Landmarks]] * [[:en:Hiking|Hiking]] * {{ill|Hiyoriyama (Toba City)|ja|日和山 (鳥羽市)|qq=|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hiyoriyama (Toba City)|qq=}} == External Links == * [http://maikoh.net/corp/event/aominezan/ Shofuku-ji Temple - Aonomine Mountain Official Page]

{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11661367]] Wikidata name: Mount Aonomine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:37, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11661367]] Wikidata name: Mount Aonomine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 02:35, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox mountain | name = Mount Aonomine | image = [[File:Mt. Aonomine from Shimonogo, Isobe.jpg|250px]] | caption = View from the Shima City side | elevation_m = 336.3 | coordinates = {{coord|34|24|32|N|136|49|27|E|type:mountain|display=inline,title|qq=}} | location = {{JPN|qq=}} [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]] ([[:en:Toba City|Toba City]] and [[:en:Shima City|Shima City]]) | range = Isolated mountain | type = Low-altitude, solitary mountain<ref>Toba City History Compilation Office (1991): Upper Volume, p.23</ref> }} '''Mount Aonomine''' (青峰山, Aonomine-san<ref>Mie Prefecture (2005) "[http://www.pref.mie.jp/KAWASABO/HP/kasen/seibi/kamokihon.pdf Basic Policy for River Improvement in the Kamo River System]" p.3</ref>) is a mountain located on the border between [[:en:Toba, Mie|Toba City]] and [[:en:Shima, Mie|Shima City]] in [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]]. It has an elevation of 336 meters. == Overview == Despite its relatively low elevation, Mount Aonomine offers clear views of [[:en:Ise Bay|Ise Bay]], [[:en:Enshū Sea|Enshū-nada Sea]], and [[:en:Kumano Sea|Kumano-nada Sea]], making it a landmark for {{ill|kaisen|ja|廻船|qq=|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kaisen|qq=|1=kaisen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11486879}} sailors in ancient times. The mountain is home to {{ill|Shofuku-ji Temple|ja|正福寺 (鳥羽市)|qq=|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shofuku-ji Temple|qq=|1=Shōfuku-ji Temple|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11545590}}, which enshrines a golden statue of {{ill|Kannon|ja|観音|qq=|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kannon|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 観音菩薩}} riding on a {{ill|whale|ja|鯨|qq=|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/whale|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to クジラ|1=whale|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1865281}}. The temple has historically been a site for maritime safety prayers. The mountain’s forests of [[:en:Castanopsis|Castanopsis]] and {{ill|Quercus|ja|カシ|qq=|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Quercus|qq=|1=Cyclobalanopsis|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q796117}} trees contribute to its scenic beauty, making it a popular spot on the {{ill|Kinki Nature Trail|ja|近畿自然歩道|qq=|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinki Nature Trail|qq=|1=Kinki Nature Trail|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11638658}}. While the advent of GPS has reduced its significance as a navigational aid, the area is preserved as a {{ill|Protective Forest for Navigation|ja|航行目標保安林|qq=|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Protective Forest for Navigation|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. The summit features an observation deck and a digital television relay station, the {{ill|Isobe Relay Station|ja|磯部中継局|qq=|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Isobe Relay Station|qq=|1=Isobe Relay Station|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11588064}}. The haiku pen name of {{ill|Seihō Shimada|ja|嶋田青峰|qq=|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seihō Shimada|qq=|1=Seihō Shimada|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11477392}}, a poet from nearby Matoya, is derived from this mountain.<ref>Hosoi (1996): p.48</ref> On May 25, 2016, Canadian Prime Minister {{ill|Justin Trudeau|en|qq=|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Justin Trudeau|qq=}} and his wife {{ill|Sophie Grégoire Trudeau|en|qq=|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sophie Grégoire Trudeau|qq=}} hiked Mount Aonomine during their visit for the {{ill|42nd G7 Summit|en|第42回先進国首脳会議|qq=|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/42nd G7 Summit|qq=}} in Ise-Shima.<ref>"Canadian Prime Minister Enjoys Hiking with His Wife on Their Wedding Anniversary," *Chunichi Shimbun*, May 26, 2016</ref> == Geography and Geology == Mount Aonomine is classified as part of the "Eastern Terrace District," a 100–115 meter hilly region within Toba City.<ref>Toba City History Compilation Office (1991): Upper Volume, p.18</ref> It serves as the source of the {{ill|Kamo River (Mie Prefecture)|ja|加茂川|qq=|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamo River (Mie Prefecture)|qq=|1=加茂川|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q16279097}}, a secondary river.<ref>Toba City History Compilation Office (1991): Upper Volume, p.20</ref> Geologically, it belongs to the {{ill|Matoya Formation|ja|的矢層群|qq=|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Matoya Formation|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, which is part of the {{ill|Shimanto Belt|ja|四万十層群|qq=|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shimanto Belt|qq=|1=Shimanto group|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11254492}}, formed during the [[:en:Mesozoic|Mesozoic Era]].<ref>Kawai (1967): p.44</ref> == Aonomine Trails == The mountain has several trails collectively known as "Aonomine Trails": * '''Matsuo Trail''': Two routes (old and new) starting from Matsuo in Toba City. * '''Kenko Trail''': A route starting in Kenko in Toba City. * '''Isobe Trail''': Starting from Izawa-no-Miya Shrine in Shima City. * '''Matoya Trail''': A drivable route from Matoya Shrine in Shima City. * '''Gochi Trail''': A pedestrian-only route starting near [[:en:Gochi Station|Gochi Station]] in Shima City. == References == {{reflist|qq=}} == Further Reading == * Isobe Town History Compilation Committee, *Isobe Town History: Upper Volume*, 1997. * Kawai Masatora, "Exploring the Origins of the Japanese Archipelago," *Geological News*, 1967. * Toba City History Compilation Office, *Toba City History: Upper and Lower Volumes*, 1991. * Hosoi Keiji, "Seihō Shimada and the Haiku Literary Movement," *Haiku Literature Quarterly*, 1996. * Mie Prefecture Board of Education, *Historical Pathways of Ise: Asama-Toba Road and Others*, 1986. == Related Articles == * [[:en:Ise-Shima National Park|Ise-Shima National Park]] * [[:en:Sea lane|Navigation]] * [[:en:Sea mark|Landmarks]] * [[:en:Hiking|Hiking]] * {{ill|Hiyoriyama (Toba City)|ja|日和山 (鳥羽市)|qq=|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hiyoriyama (Toba City)|qq=|1=Mount Hiyori|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11505197}} == External Links == * [http://maikoh.net/corp/event/aominezan/ Shofuku-ji Temple - Aonomine Mountain Official Page]

{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11661367]] Wikidata name: Mount Aonomine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:37, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11661367]] Wikidata name: Mount Aonomine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 02:35, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox mountain | name = Mount Aonomine | image = [[File:Mt. Aonomine from Shimonogo, Isobe.jpg|250px]] | caption = View from the Shima City side | elevation_m = 336.3 | coordinates = {{coord|34|24|32|N|136|49|27|E|type:mountain|display=inline,title|qq=}} | location = {{JPN|qq=}} [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]] ([[:en:Toba City|Toba City]] and [[:en:Shima City|Shima City]]) | range = Isolated mountain | type = Low-altitude, solitary mountain<ref>Toba City History Compilation Office (1991): Upper Volume, p.23</ref> }} '''Mount Aonomine''' (青峰山, Aonomine-san<ref>Mie Prefecture (2005) "[http://www.pref.mie.jp/KAWASABO/HP/kasen/seibi/kamokihon.pdf Basic Policy for River Improvement in the Kamo River System]" p.3</ref>) is a mountain located on the border between [[:en:Toba, Mie|Toba City]] and [[:en:Shima, Mie|Shima City]] in [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]]. It has an elevation of 336 meters. == Overview == Despite its relatively low elevation, Mount Aonomine offers clear views of [[:en:Ise Bay|Ise Bay]], [[:en:Enshū Sea|Enshū-nada Sea]], and [[:en:Kumano Sea|Kumano-nada Sea]], making it a landmark for {{ill|kaisen|ja|廻船|qq=|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kaisen|qq=|1=kaisen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11486879}} sailors in ancient times. The mountain is home to {{ill|Shofuku-ji Temple|ja|正福寺 (鳥羽市)|qq=|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shofuku-ji Temple|qq=|1=Shōfuku-ji Temple|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11545590}}, which enshrines a golden statue of {{ill|Kannon|ja|観音|qq=|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kannon|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 観音菩薩}} riding on a {{ill|whale|ja|鯨|qq=|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/whale|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to クジラ|1=whale|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1865281}}. The temple has historically been a site for maritime safety prayers. The mountain’s forests of [[:en:Castanopsis|Castanopsis]] and {{ill|Quercus|ja|カシ|qq=|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Quercus|qq=|1=Cyclobalanopsis|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q796117}} trees contribute to its scenic beauty, making it a popular spot on the {{ill|Kinki Nature Trail|ja|近畿自然歩道|qq=|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinki Nature Trail|qq=|1=Kinki Nature Trail|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11638658}}. While the advent of GPS has reduced its significance as a navigational aid, the area is preserved as a {{ill|Protective Forest for Navigation|ja|航行目標保安林|qq=|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Protective Forest for Navigation|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=Q135503346}}. The summit features an observation deck and a digital television relay station, the {{ill|Isobe Relay Station|ja|磯部中継局|qq=|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Isobe Relay Station|qq=|1=Isobe Relay Station|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11588064}}. The haiku pen name of {{ill|Seihō Shimada|ja|嶋田青峰|qq=|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seihō Shimada|qq=|1=Seihō Shimada|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11477392}}, a poet from nearby Matoya, is derived from this mountain.<ref>Hosoi (1996): p.48</ref> On May 25, 2016, Canadian Prime Minister {{ill|Justin Trudeau|en|qq=|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Justin Trudeau|qq=}} and his wife {{ill|Sophie Grégoire Trudeau|en|qq=|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sophie Grégoire Trudeau|qq=}} hiked Mount Aonomine during their visit for the {{ill|42nd G7 Summit|en|第42回先進国首脳会議|qq=|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/42nd G7 Summit|qq=}} in Ise-Shima.<ref>"Canadian Prime Minister Enjoys Hiking with His Wife on Their Wedding Anniversary," *Chunichi Shimbun*, May 26, 2016</ref> == Geography and Geology == Mount Aonomine is classified as part of the "Eastern Terrace District," a 100–115 meter hilly region within Toba City.<ref>Toba City History Compilation Office (1991): Upper Volume, p.18</ref> It serves as the source of the {{ill|Kamo River (Mie Prefecture)|ja|加茂川|qq=|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamo River (Mie Prefecture)|qq=|1=加茂川|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q16279097}}, a secondary river.<ref>Toba City History Compilation Office (1991): Upper Volume, p.20</ref> Geologically, it belongs to the {{ill|Matoya Formation|ja|的矢層群|qq=|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Matoya Formation|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, which is part of the {{ill|Shimanto Belt|ja|四万十層群|qq=|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shimanto Belt|qq=|1=Shimanto group|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11254492}}, formed during the [[:en:Mesozoic|Mesozoic Era]].<ref>Kawai (1967): p.44</ref> == Aonomine Trails == The mountain has several trails collectively known as "Aonomine Trails": * '''Matsuo Trail''': Two routes (old and new) starting from Matsuo in Toba City. * '''Kenko Trail''': A route starting in Kenko in Toba City. * '''Isobe Trail''': Starting from Izawa-no-Miya Shrine in Shima City. * '''Matoya Trail''': A drivable route from Matoya Shrine in Shima City. * '''Gochi Trail''': A pedestrian-only route starting near [[:en:Gochi Station|Gochi Station]] in Shima City. == References == {{reflist|qq=}} == Further Reading == * Isobe Town History Compilation Committee, *Isobe Town History: Upper Volume*, 1997. * Kawai Masatora, "Exploring the Origins of the Japanese Archipelago," *Geological News*, 1967. * Toba City History Compilation Office, *Toba City History: Upper and Lower Volumes*, 1991. * Hosoi Keiji, "Seihō Shimada and the Haiku Literary Movement," *Haiku Literature Quarterly*, 1996. * Mie Prefecture Board of Education, *Historical Pathways of Ise: Asama-Toba Road and Others*, 1986. == Related Articles == * [[:en:Ise-Shima National Park|Ise-Shima National Park]] * [[:en:Sea lane|Navigation]] * [[:en:Sea mark|Landmarks]] * [[:en:Hiking|Hiking]] * {{ill|Hiyoriyama (Toba City)|ja|日和山 (鳥羽市)|qq=|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hiyoriyama (Toba City)|qq=|1=Mount Hiyori|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11505197}} == External Links == * [http://maikoh.net/corp/event/aominezan/ Shofuku-ji Temple - Aonomine Mountain Official Page]

* Toba City History Compilation Office, *Toba City History: Upper and Lower Volumes*, 1991. * Hosoi Keiji, "Seihō Shimada and the Haiku Literary Movement," *Haiku Literature Quarterly*, 1996. * Mie Prefecture Board of Education, *Historical Pathways of Ise: Asama-Toba Road and Others*, 1986. == Related Articles == * [[:en:Ise-Shima National Park|Ise-Shima National Park]] * [[:en:Sea lane|Navigation]] * [[:en:Sea mark|Landmarks]] * [[:en:Hiking|Hiking]] * {{ill|Hiyoriyama (Toba City)|ja|日和山 (鳥羽市)|qq=|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hiyoriyama (Toba City)|qq=|1=Mount Hiyori|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11505197}} == External Links revision.comment: Bot: categorize based on Wikidata P11250 property
revision.text:
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11661367]] Wikidata name: Mount Aonomine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:37, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11661367]] Wikidata name: Mount Aonomine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 02:35, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox mountain | name = Mount Aonomine | image = [[File:Mt. Aonomine from Shimonogo, Isobe.jpg|250px]] | caption = View from the Shima City side | elevation_m = 336.3 | coordinates = {{coord|34|24|32|N|136|49|27|E|type:mountain|display=inline,title|qq=}} | location = {{JPN|qq=}} [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]] ([[:en:Toba City|Toba City]] and [[:en:Shima City|Shima City]]) | range = Isolated mountain | type = Low-altitude, solitary mountain<ref>Toba City History Compilation Office (1991): Upper Volume, p.23</ref> }} '''Mount Aonomine''' (青峰山, Aonomine-san<ref>Mie Prefecture (2005) "[http://www.pref.mie.jp/KAWASABO/HP/kasen/seibi/kamokihon.pdf Basic Policy for River Improvement in the Kamo River System]" p.3</ref>) is a mountain located on the border between [[:en:Toba, Mie|Toba City]] and [[:en:Shima, Mie|Shima City]] in [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]]. It has an elevation of 336 meters. == Overview == Despite its relatively low elevation, Mount Aonomine offers clear views of [[:en:Ise Bay|Ise Bay]], [[:en:Enshū Sea|Enshū-nada Sea]], and [[:en:Kumano Sea|Kumano-nada Sea]], making it a landmark for {{ill|kaisen|ja|廻船|qq=|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kaisen|qq=|1=kaisen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11486879}} sailors in ancient times. The mountain is home to {{ill|Shofuku-ji Temple|ja|正福寺 (鳥羽市)|qq=|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shofuku-ji Temple|qq=|1=Shōfuku-ji Temple|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11545590}}, which enshrines a golden statue of {{ill|Kannon|ja|観音|qq=|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kannon|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 観音菩薩}} riding on a {{ill|whale|ja|鯨|qq=|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/whale|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to クジラ|1=whale|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1865281}}. The temple has historically been a site for maritime safety prayers. The mountain’s forests of [[:en:Castanopsis|Castanopsis]] and {{ill|Quercus|ja|カシ|qq=|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Quercus|qq=|1=Cyclobalanopsis|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q796117}} trees contribute to its scenic beauty, making it a popular spot on the {{ill|Kinki Nature Trail|ja|近畿自然歩道|qq=|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinki Nature Trail|qq=|1=Kinki Nature Trail|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11638658}}. While the advent of GPS has reduced its significance as a navigational aid, the area is preserved as a {{ill|Protective Forest for Navigation|ja|航行目標保安林|qq=|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Protective Forest for Navigation|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=Q135503346}}. The summit features an observation deck and a digital television relay station, the {{ill|Isobe Relay Station|ja|磯部中継局|qq=|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Isobe Relay Station|qq=|1=Isobe Relay Station|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11588064}}. The haiku pen name of {{ill|Seihō Shimada|ja|嶋田青峰|qq=|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seihō Shimada|qq=|1=Seihō Shimada|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11477392}}, a poet from nearby Matoya, is derived from this mountain.<ref>Hosoi (1996): p.48</ref> On May 25, 2016, Canadian Prime Minister {{ill|Justin Trudeau|en|qq=|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Justin Trudeau|qq=}} and his wife {{ill|Sophie Grégoire Trudeau|en|qq=|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sophie Grégoire Trudeau|qq=}} hiked Mount Aonomine during their visit for the {{ill|42nd G7 Summit|en|第42回先進国首脳会議|qq=|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/42nd G7 Summit|qq=}} in Ise-Shima.<ref>"Canadian Prime Minister Enjoys Hiking with His Wife on Their Wedding Anniversary," *Chunichi Shimbun*, May 26, 2016</ref> == Geography and Geology == Mount Aonomine is classified as part of the "Eastern Terrace District," a 100–115 meter hilly region within Toba City.<ref>Toba City History Compilation Office (1991): Upper Volume, p.18</ref> It serves as the source of the {{ill|Kamo River (Mie Prefecture)|ja|加茂川|qq=|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamo River (Mie Prefecture)|qq=|1=加茂川|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q16279097}}, a secondary river.<ref>Toba City History Compilation Office (1991): Upper Volume, p.20</ref> Geologically, it belongs to the {{ill|Matoya Formation|ja|的矢層群|qq=|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Matoya Formation|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, which is part of the {{ill|Shimanto Belt|ja|四万十層群|qq=|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shimanto Belt|qq=|1=Shimanto group|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11254492}}, formed during the [[:en:Mesozoic|Mesozoic Era]].<ref>Kawai (1967): p.44</ref> == Aonomine Trails == The mountain has several trails collectively known as "Aonomine Trails": * '''Matsuo Trail''': Two routes (old and new) starting from Matsuo in Toba City. * '''Kenko Trail''': A route starting in Kenko in Toba City. * '''Isobe Trail''': Starting from Izawa-no-Miya Shrine in Shima City. * '''Matoya Trail''': A drivable route from Matoya Shrine in Shima City. * '''Gochi Trail''': A pedestrian-only route starting near [[:en:Gochi Station|Gochi Station]] in Shima City. == References == {{reflist|qq=}} == Further Reading == * Isobe Town History Compilation Committee, *Isobe Town History: Upper Volume*, 1997. * Kawai Masatora, "Exploring the Origins of the Japanese Archipelago," *Geological News*, 1967. * Toba City History Compilation Office, *Toba City History: Upper and Lower Volumes*, 1991. * Hosoi Keiji, "Seihō Shimada and the Haiku Literary Movement," *Haiku Literature Quarterly*, 1996. * Mie Prefecture Board of Education, *Historical Pathways of Ise: Asama-Toba Road and Others*, 1986. == Related Articles == * [[:en:Ise-Shima National Park|Ise-Shima National Park]] * [[:en:Sea lane|Navigation]] * [[:en:Sea mark|Landmarks]] * [[:en:Hiking|Hiking]] * {{ill|Hiyoriyama (Toba City)|ja|日和山 (鳥羽市)|qq=|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hiyoriyama (Toba City)|qq=|1=Mount Hiyori|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11505197}} == External Links == * [http://maikoh.net/corp/event/aominezan/ Shofuku-ji Temple - Aonomine Mountain Official Page]

Prefecture)|ja|加茂川|qq=|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamo River (Mie Prefecture)|qq=|1=加茂川|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q16279097}}, a secondary river.<ref>Toba City History Compilation Office (1991): Upper Volume, p.20</ref> Geologically, it belongs to the {{ill|Matoya Formation|ja|的矢層群|qq=|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Matoya Formation|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, which is part of the {{ill|Shimanto Belt|ja|四万十層群|qq=|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shimanto Belt|qq=|1=Shimanto group|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11254492}}, formed during the [[:en:Mesozoic|Mesozoic Era]].<ref>Kawai (1967): p.44</ref> == Aonomine Trails == The mountain has several trails collectively known as "Aonomine Trails": * '''Matsuo Trail''': Two routes (old and new) starting from Matsuo in Toba City. * '''Kenko Trail''': A route starting in Kenko in Toba City. * '''Isobe Trail''': Starting from Izawa-no-Miya Shrine in Shima City. * '''Matoya Trail''': A drivable route from Matoya Shrine in Shima City. * '''Gochi Trail''': A pedestrian-only route starting near [[:en:Gochi Station|Gochi Station]] in Shima City. == References == {{reflist|qq=}} == Further Reading == * Isobe Town History Compilation Committee, *Isobe Town History: Upper Volume*, 1997. * Kawai Masatora, "Exploring the Origins of the Japanese Archipelago," *Geological News*, 1967. * Toba City History Compilation Office, *Toba City History: Upper and Lower Volumes*, 1991. * Hosoi Keiji, "Seihō Shimada and the Haiku Literary Movement," *Haiku Literature Quarterly*, 1996. * Mie <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ceb:Aomine-yama]][[ja:青峰山]][[lld:Mont Aonomine]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11661367]] Wikidata name: Mount Aonomine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:37, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11661367]] Wikidata name: Mount Aonomine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 02:35, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox mountain | name = Mount Aonomine | image = [[File:Mt. Aonomine from Shimonogo, Isobe.jpg|250px]] | caption = View from the Shima City side | elevation_m = 336.3 | coordinates = {{coord|34|24|32|N|136|49|27|E|type:mountain|display=inline,title|qq=}} | location = {{JPN|qq=}} [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]] ([[:en:Toba City|Toba City]] and [[:en:Shima City|Shima City]]) | range = Isolated mountain | type = Low-altitude, solitary mountain<ref>Toba City History Compilation Office (1991): Upper Volume, p.23</ref> }} '''Mount Aonomine''' (青峰山, Aonomine-san<ref>Mie Prefecture (2005) "[http://www.pref.mie.jp/KAWASABO/HP/kasen/seibi/kamokihon.pdf Basic Policy for River Improvement in the Kamo River System]" p.3</ref>) is a mountain located on the border between [[:en:Toba, Mie|Toba City]] and [[:en:Shima, Mie|Shima City]] in [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]]. It has an elevation of 336 meters. == Overview == Despite its relatively low elevation, Mount Aonomine offers clear views of [[:en:Ise Bay|Ise Bay]], [[:en:Enshū Sea|Enshū-nada Sea]], and [[:en:Kumano Sea|Kumano-nada Sea]], making it a landmark for {{ill|kaisen|ja|廻船|qq=|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kaisen|qq=|1=kaisen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11486879}} sailors in ancient times. The mountain is home to {{ill|Shofuku-ji Temple|ja|正福寺 (鳥羽市)|qq=|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shofuku-ji Temple|qq=|1=Shōfuku-ji Temple|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11545590}}, which enshrines a golden statue of {{ill|Kannon|ja|観音|qq=|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kannon|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 観音菩薩}} riding on a {{ill|whale|ja|鯨|qq=|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/whale|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to クジラ|1=whale|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1865281}}. The temple has historically been a site for maritime safety prayers. The mountain’s forests of [[:en:Castanopsis|Castanopsis]] and {{ill|Quercus|ja|カシ|qq=|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Quercus|qq=|1=Cyclobalanopsis|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q796117}} trees contribute to its scenic beauty, making it a popular spot on the {{ill|Kinki Nature Trail|ja|近畿自然歩道|qq=|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinki Nature Trail|qq=|1=Kinki Nature Trail|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11638658}}. While the advent of GPS has reduced its significance as a navigational aid, the area is preserved as a {{ill|Protective Forest for Navigation|ja|航行目標保安林|qq=|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Protective Forest for Navigation|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|WD=Q135503346}}. The summit features an observation deck and a digital television relay station, the {{ill|Isobe Relay Station|ja|磯部中継局|qq=|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Isobe Relay Station|qq=|1=Isobe Relay Station|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11588064}}. The haiku pen name of {{ill|Seihō Shimada|ja|嶋田青峰|qq=|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seihō Shimada|qq=|1=Seihō Shimada|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11477392}}, a poet from nearby Matoya, is derived from this mountain.<ref>Hosoi (1996): p.48</ref> On May 25, 2016, Canadian Prime Minister {{ill|Justin Trudeau|en|qq=|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Justin Trudeau|qq=}} and his wife {{ill|Sophie Grégoire Trudeau|en|qq=|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sophie Grégoire Trudeau|qq=}} hiked Mount Aonomine during their visit for the {{ill|42nd G7 Summit|en|第42回先進国首脳会議|qq=|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/42nd G7 Summit|qq=}} in Ise-Shima.<ref>"Canadian Prime Minister Enjoys Hiking with His Wife on Their Wedding Anniversary," *Chunichi Shimbun*, May 26, 2016</ref> == Geography and Geology == Mount Aonomine is classified as part of the "Eastern Terrace District," a 100–115 meter hilly region within Toba City.<ref>Toba City History Compilation Office (1991): Upper Volume, p.18</ref> It serves as the source of the {{ill|Kamo River (Mie Prefecture)|ja|加茂川|qq=|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamo River (Mie Prefecture)|qq=|1=加茂川|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q16279097}}, a secondary river.<ref>Toba City History Compilation Office (1991): Upper Volume, p.20</ref> Geologically, it belongs to the {{ill|Matoya Formation|ja|的矢層群|qq=|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Matoya Formation|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, which is part of the {{ill|Shimanto Belt|ja|四万十層群|qq=|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shimanto Belt|qq=|1=Shimanto group|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11254492}}, formed during the [[:en:Mesozoic|Mesozoic Era]].<ref>Kawai (1967): p.44</ref> == Aonomine Trails == The mountain has several trails collectively known as "Aonomine Trails": * '''Matsuo Trail''': Two routes (old and new) starting from Matsuo in Toba City. * '''Kenko Trail''': A route starting in Kenko in Toba City. * '''Isobe Trail''': Starting from Izawa-no-Miya Shrine in Shima City. * '''Matoya Trail''': A drivable route from Matoya Shrine in Shima City. * '''Gochi Trail''': A pedestrian-only route starting near [[:en:Gochi Station|Gochi Station]] in Shima City. == References == {{reflist|qq=}} == Prefecture Board of Education, *Historical Pathways of Ise: Asama-Toba Road and Others*, 1986. == Related Articles == * [[:en:Ise-Shima National Park|Ise-Shima National Park]] * [[:en:Sea The haiku pen name of {{ill|Seihō Shimada|ja|嶋田青峰|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seihō Shimada|1=Seihō Shimada|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11477392}}, a poet from nearby Matoya, is derived from this mountain.<ref>Hosoi (1996): p.48</ref> On May 25, 2016, Canadian Prime Minister {{ill|Justin Trudeau|en|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Justin Trudeau|qq=}} and his wife {{ill|Sophie Grégoire Trudeau|en|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sophie Grégoire Trudeau|qq=}} hiked Mount Aonomine during their visit for the {{ill|42nd G7 Summit|en|第42回先進国首脳会議|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/42nd G7 Summit|qq=}} in Ise-Shima.<ref>"Canadian Prime Minister Enjoys Hiking with His Wife on Their Wedding Anniversary," *Chunichi Shimbun*, May 26, 2016</ref> == Geography and Geology == Mount Aonomine is classified as part of the "Eastern Terrace District," a 100–115 meter hilly region within Toba City.<ref>Toba City History Compilation Office (1991): Upper Volume, p.18</ref> It serves as the source of the {{ill|Kamo River (Mie Prefecture)|ja|加茂川|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamo River (Mie Prefecture)|1=加茂川|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q16279097}}, a secondary river.<ref>Toba City History Compilation Office (1991): Upper Volume, p.20</ref> Geologically, it belongs to the {{ill|Matoya Formation|ja|的矢層群|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Matoya Formation|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, which is part of the {{ill|Shimanto Belt|ja|四万十層群|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shimanto Belt|1=Shimanto group|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11254492}}, formed during the [[:en:Mesozoic|Mesozoic Era]].<ref>Kawai (1967): p.44</ref> == Aonomine Trails == The mountain has several trails collectively known as "Aonomine Trails": * '''Matsuo Trail''': Two routes (old and new) starting from Matsuo in Toba City. * '''Kenko Trail''': A route starting in Kenko in Toba City. * '''Isobe Trail''': Starting from Izawa-no-Miya Shrine in Shima City. * '''Matoya Trail''': A drivable route from Matoya Shrine in Shima City. * '''Gochi Trail''': A pedestrian-only route starting near [[:en:Gochi Station|Gochi Station]] in Shima City. == References == {{reflist|qq=}} == <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ceb:Aomine-yama]][[ja:青峰山]][[lld:Mont Aonomine]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11661367]] Wikidata name: Mount Aonomine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:37, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11661367]] Wikidata name: Mount Aonomine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 02:35, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox mountain | name = Mount Aonomine | image = [[File:Mt. Aonomine from Shimonogo, Isobe.jpg|250px]] | caption = View from the Shima City side | elevation_m = 336.3 | coordinates = {{coord|34|24|32|N|136|49|27|E|type:mountain|display=inline,title|qq=}} | location = {{JPN|qq=}} [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]] ([[:en:Toba City|Toba City]] and [[:en:Shima City|Shima City]]) | range = Isolated mountain | type = Low-altitude, solitary mountain<ref>Toba City History Compilation Office (1991): Upper Volume, p.23</ref> }} '''Mount Aonomine''' (青峰山, Aonomine-san<ref>Mie Prefecture (2005) "[http://www.pref.mie.jp/KAWASABO/HP/kasen/seibi/kamokihon.pdf Basic Policy for River Improvement in the Kamo River System]" p.3</ref>) is a mountain located on the border between [[:en:Toba, Mie|Toba City]] and [[:en:Shima, Mie|Shima City]] in [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]]. It has an elevation of 336 meters. == Overview == Despite its relatively low elevation, Mount Aonomine offers clear views of [[:en:Ise Bay|Ise Bay]], [[:en:Enshū Sea|Enshū-nada Sea]], and [[:en:Kumano Sea|Kumano-nada Sea]], making it a landmark for {{ill|kaisen|ja|廻船|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kaisen|1=kaisen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11486879}} sailors in ancient times. The mountain is home to {{ill|Shofuku-ji Temple|ja|正福寺 (鳥羽市)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shofuku-ji Temple|1=Shōfuku-ji Temple|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11545590}}, which enshrines a golden statue of {{ill|Kannon|ja|観音|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kannon|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 観音菩薩}} riding on a {{ill|whale|ja|鯨|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/whale|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to クジラ|1=whale|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1865281}}. The temple has historically been a site for maritime safety prayers. The mountain’s forests of [[:en:Castanopsis|Castanopsis]] and {{ill|Quercus|ja|カシ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Quercus|1=Cyclobalanopsis|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q796117}} trees contribute to its scenic beauty, making it a popular spot on the {{ill|Kinki Nature Trail|ja|近畿自然歩道|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinki Nature Trail|1=Kinki Nature Trail|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11638658}}. While the advent of GPS has reduced its significance as a navigational aid, the area is preserved as a {{ill|Protective Forest for Navigation|ja|航行目標保安林|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Protective Forest for Navigation|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|WD=Q135503346}}. The summit features an observation deck and a digital television relay station, the {{ill|Isobe Relay Station|ja|磯部中継局|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Isobe Relay Station|1=Isobe Relay Station|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11588064}}. The haiku pen name of {{ill|Seihō Shimada|ja|嶋田青峰|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seihō Shimada|1=Seihō Shimada|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11477392}}, a poet from nearby Matoya, is derived from this mountain.<ref>Hosoi (1996): p.48</ref> On May 25, 2016, Canadian Prime Minister {{ill|Justin Trudeau|en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Justin Trudeau|qq=}} and his wife {{ill|Sophie Grégoire Trudeau|en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sophie Grégoire Trudeau|qq=}} hiked Mount Aonomine during their visit for the {{ill|42nd G7 Summit|en|第42回先進国首脳会議|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/42nd G7 Summit|qq=}} in Ise-Shima.<ref>"Canadian Prime Minister Enjoys Hiking with His Wife on Their Wedding Anniversary," *Chunichi Shimbun*, May 26, 2016</ref> == Geography and Geology == Mount Aonomine is classified as part of the "Eastern Terrace District," a 100–115 meter hilly region within Toba City.<ref>Toba City History Compilation Office (1991): Upper Volume, p.18</ref> It serves as the source of the {{ill|Kamo River (Mie Prefecture)|ja|加茂川|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamo River (Mie Prefecture)|1=加茂川|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q16279097}}, a secondary river.<ref>Toba City History Compilation Office (1991): Upper Volume, p.20</ref> Geologically, it belongs to the {{ill|Matoya Formation|ja|的矢層群|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Matoya Formation|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, which is part of the {{ill|Shimanto Belt|ja|四万十層群|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shimanto Belt|1=Shimanto group|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11254492}}, formed during the [[:en:Mesozoic|Mesozoic Summit|qq=}} in Ise-Shima.<ref>"Canadian Prime Minister Enjoys Hiking with His Wife on Their Wedding Anniversary," *Chunichi Shimbun*, May 26, 2016</ref> == Geography and Geology == Mount Aonomine is classified as part of the "Eastern Terrace District," a 100–115 meter hilly region within Toba City.<ref>Toba City History Compilation Office (1991): Upper Volume, p.18</ref> It serves as the source of the {{ill|Kamo River (Mie Prefecture)|ja|加茂川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamo River (Mie Prefecture)|1=加茂川|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q16279097}}, a secondary river.<ref>Toba City History Compilation Office (1991): Upper Volume, p.20</ref> Geologically, it belongs to the {{ill|Matoya Formation|ja|的矢層群|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Matoya Formation|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, which is part of the {{ill|Shimanto Belt|ja|四万十層群|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shimanto Belt|1=Shimanto group|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11254492}}, formed during the [[:en:Mesozoic|Mesozoic Era]].<ref>Kawai (1967): p.44</ref> == Aonomine Trails == The mountain has several trails collectively known as "Aonomine Trails": * '''Matsuo Trail''': Two routes (old and new) starting from Matsuo in Toba City. * '''Kenko Trail''': A route starting in Kenko in Toba City. * '''Isobe Trail''': Starting from Izawa-no-Miya Shrine in Shima City. * '''Matoya Trail''': A drivable route from Matoya Shrine in Shima City. * '''Gochi Trail''': A pedestrian-only route starting near [[:en:Gochi Station|Gochi Station]] in Shima City. == References == {{reflist|qq=}} == Further Reading == * Isobe Town History Compilation Committee, *Isobe Town History: Upper Volume*, 1997. * Kawai Masatora, "Exploring the Origins of the Japanese Archipelago," *Geological News*, 1967. * Toba City History Compilation Office, *Toba City History: Upper and Lower Volumes*, 1991. * Hosoi Keiji, "Seihō Shimada and the Haiku Literary Movement," *Haiku Literature Quarterly*, 1996. * Mie Prefecture Board of Education, *Historical Pathways of Ise: Asama-Toba Road and Others*, 1986. == Related Articles == * [[:en:Ise-Shima National Park|Ise-Shima National Park]] * [[:en:Sea lane|Navigation]] * [[:en:Sea mark|Landmarks]] * [[:en:Hiking|Hiking]] * {{ill|Hiyoriyama (Toba City)|ja|日和山 (鳥羽市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hiyoriyama (Toba City)|1=Mount Hiyori|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11505197}} == External Links == * [http://maikoh.net/corp/event/aominezan/ Shofuku-ji Temple - Aonomine Mountain Official Page]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ceb:Aomine-yama]][[ja:青峰山]][[lld:Mont Aonomine]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11661367]] Wikidata name: Mount Aonomine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:37, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11661367]] Wikidata name: Mount Aonomine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 02:35, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox mountain | name = Mount Aonomine | image = [[File:Mt. Aonomine from Shimonogo, Isobe.jpg|250px]] | caption = View from the Shima City side | elevation_m = 336.3 | coordinates = {{coord|34|24|32|N|136|49|27|E|type:mountain|display=inline,title|qq=}} | location = {{JPN|qq=}} [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]] ([[:en:Toba City|Toba City]] and [[:en:Shima City|Shima City]]) | range = Isolated mountain | type = Low-altitude, solitary mountain<ref>Toba City History Compilation Office (1991): Upper Volume, p.23</ref> }} '''Mount Aonomine''' (青峰山, Aonomine-san<ref>Mie Prefecture (2005) "[http://www.pref.mie.jp/KAWASABO/HP/kasen/seibi/kamokihon.pdf Basic Policy for River Improvement in the Kamo River System]" p.3</ref>) is a mountain located on the border between [[:en:Toba, Mie|Toba City]] and [[:en:Shima, Mie|Shima City]] in [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]]. It has an elevation of 336 meters. == Overview == Despite its relatively low elevation, Mount Aonomine offers clear views of [[:en:Ise Bay|Ise Bay]], [[:en:Enshū Sea|Enshū-nada Sea]], and [[:en:Kumano Sea|Kumano-nada Sea]], making it a landmark for {{ill|kaisen|ja|廻船|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kaisen|1=kaisen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11486879}} sailors in ancient times. The mountain is home to {{ill|Shofuku-ji Temple|ja|正福寺 (鳥羽市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shofuku-ji Temple|1=Shōfuku-ji Temple|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11545590}}, which enshrines a golden statue of {{ill|Kannon|ja|観音|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kannon|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 観音菩薩}} riding on a {{ill|whale|ja|鯨|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/whale|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to クジラ|1=whale|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1865281}}. The temple has historically been a site for maritime safety prayers. The mountain’s forests of [[:en:Castanopsis|Castanopsis]] and {{ill|Quercus|ja|カシ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Quercus|1=Cyclobalanopsis|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q796117}} trees contribute to its scenic beauty, making it a popular spot on the {{ill|Kinki Nature Trail|ja|近畿自然歩道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinki Nature Trail|1=Kinki Nature Trail|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11638658}}. While the advent of GPS has reduced its significance as a navigational aid, the area is preserved as a {{ill|Protective Forest for Navigation|ja|航行目標保安林|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Protective Forest for Navigation|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|WD=Q135503346}}. The summit features an observation deck and a digital television relay station, the {{ill|Isobe Relay Station|ja|磯部中継局|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Isobe Relay Station|1=Isobe Relay Station|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11588064}}. The haiku pen name of {{ill|Seihō Shimada|ja|嶋田青峰|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seihō Shimada|1=Seihō Shimada|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11477392}}, a poet from nearby Matoya, is derived from this mountain.<ref>Hosoi (1996): p.48</ref> On May 25, 2016, Canadian Prime Minister {{ill|Justin Trudeau|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Justin Trudeau|qq=}} and his wife {{ill|Sophie Grégoire Trudeau|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sophie Grégoire Trudeau|qq=}} hiked Mount Aonomine during their visit for the {{ill|42nd G7 Summit|en|第42回先進国首脳会議|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/42nd G7 Summit|qq=}} in Ise-Shima.<ref>"Canadian Prime Minister Enjoys Hiking with His Wife on Their Wedding Anniversary," *Chunichi Shimbun*, May 26, 2016</ref> == Geography and Geology == Mount Aonomine is classified as part of the "Eastern Terrace District," a 100–115 meter hilly region within Toba City.<ref>Toba City History Compilation Office (1991): Upper Volume, p.18</ref> It serves as the source of the {{ill|Kamo River (Mie Prefecture)|ja|加茂川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamo River (Mie Prefecture)|1=加茂川|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q16279097}}, a secondary river.<ref>Toba City History Compilation Office (1991): Upper Volume, p.20</ref> Geologically, it belongs to the {{ill|Matoya Formation|ja|的矢層群|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Matoya Formation|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, which is part of the {{ill|Shimanto Belt|ja|四万十層群|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shimanto Belt|1=Shimanto group|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11254492}}, formed during the [[:en:Mesozoic|Mesozoic Era]].<ref>Kawai (1967): p.44</ref> == Aonomine Trails == The mountain has several trails collectively known as "Aonomine Trails": * '''Matsuo Trail''': Two routes (old and new) starting from Matsuo in Toba City. * '''Kenko Trail''': A route starting in Kenko in Toba City. * '''Isobe Trail''': Starting from Izawa-no-Miya Shrine in Shima City. * '''Matoya Trail''': A drivable route from Matoya Shrine in Shima City. * '''Gochi Trail''': A pedestrian-only route starting near [[:en:Gochi Station|Gochi Station]] in Shima City. == References == {{reflist|qq=}} == Further Reading == * Isobe Town History Compilation Committee, *Isobe Town History: Upper Volume*, 1997. * Kawai Masatora, "Exploring the Origins of the Japanese Archipelago," *Geological News*, 1967. * Toba City History Compilation Office, *Toba City History: Upper and Lower Volumes*, 1991. * Hosoi Keiji, "Seihō Shimada and the Haiku Literary Movement," *Haiku Literature Quarterly*, 1996. * Mie Prefecture Board of Education, *Historical Pathways of Ise: Asama-Toba Road and Others*, 1986. == Related Articles == * [[:en:Ise-Shima National Park|Ise-Shima National Park]] * [[:en:Sea lane|Navigation]] * [[:en:Sea mark|Landmarks]] * [[:en:Hiking|Hiking]] * {{ill|Hiyoriyama (Toba City)|ja|日和山 (鳥羽市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hiyoriyama (Toba City)|1=Mount Hiyori|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11505197}} == External Links == * [http://maikoh.net/corp/event/aominezan/ Shofuku-ji Temple - Aonomine Mountain Official Page]

* Toba City History Compilation Office, *Toba City History: Upper and Lower Volumes*, 1991. * Hosoi Keiji, "Seihō Shimada and the Haiku Literary Movement," *Haiku Literature Quarterly*, 1996. * Mie Prefecture Board of Education, *Historical Pathways of Ise: Asama-Toba Road and Others*, 1986. == Related Articles == * [[:en:Ise-Shima National Park|Ise-Shima National Park]] * [[:en:Sea lane|Navigation]] * [[:en:Sea mark|Landmarks]] * [[:en:Hiking|Hiking]] * {{ill|Hiyoriyama (Toba City)|ja|日和山 (鳥羽市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hiyoriyama (Toba City)|1=Mount Hiyori|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11505197}} == External Links == * [http://maikoh.net/corp/event/aominezan/ Shofuku-ji Temple - Aonomine Mountain Official Page]

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ceb:Aomine-yama]][[ja:青峰山]][[lld:Mont Aonomine]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11661367]] Wikidata name: Mount Aonomine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:37, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11661367]] Wikidata name: Mount Aonomine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 02:35, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox mountain | name = Mount Aonomine | image = [[File:Mt. Aonomine from Shimonogo, Isobe.jpg|250px]] | caption = View from the Shima City side | elevation_m = 336.3 | coordinates = {{coord|34|24|32|N|136|49|27|E|type:mountain|display=inline,title|qq=}} | location = {{JPN|qq=}} [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]] ([[:en:Toba City|Toba City]] and [[:en:Shima City|Shima City]]) | range = Isolated mountain | type = Low-altitude, solitary mountain<ref>Toba City History Compilation Office (1991): Upper Volume, p.23</ref> }} '''Mount Aonomine''' (青峰山, Aonomine-san<ref>Mie Prefecture (2005) "[http://www.pref.mie.jp/KAWASABO/HP/kasen/seibi/kamokihon.pdf Basic Policy for River Improvement in the Kamo River System]" p.3</ref>) is a mountain located on the border between [[:en:Toba, Mie|Toba City]] and [[:en:Shima, Mie|Shima City]] in [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]]. It has an elevation of 336 meters. == Overview == Despite its relatively low elevation, Mount Aonomine offers clear views of [[:en:Ise Bay|Ise Bay]], [[:en:Enshū Sea|Enshū-nada Sea]], and [[:en:Kumano Sea|Kumano-nada Sea]], making it a landmark for {{ill|kaisen|ja|廻船|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kaisen|1=kaisen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11486879}} sailors in ancient times. The mountain is home to {{ill|Shofuku-ji Temple|ja|正福寺 (鳥羽市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shofuku-ji Temple|1=Shōfuku-ji Temple|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11545590}}, which enshrines a golden statue of {{ill|Kannon|ja|観音|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kannon|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 観音菩薩}} riding on a {{ill|whale|ja|鯨|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/whale|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to クジラ|1=whale|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1865281}}. The temple has historically been a site for maritime safety prayers. The mountain’s forests of [[:en:Castanopsis|Castanopsis]] and {{ill|Quercus|ja|カシ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Quercus|1=Cyclobalanopsis|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q796117}} trees contribute to its scenic beauty, making it a popular spot on the {{ill|Kinki Nature Trail|ja|近畿自然歩道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinki Nature Trail|1=Kinki Nature Trail|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11638658}}. While the advent of GPS has reduced its significance as a navigational aid, the area is preserved as a {{ill|Protective Forest for Navigation|ja|航行目標保安林|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Protective Forest for Navigation|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|WD=Q135503346}}. The summit features an observation deck and a digital television relay station, the {{ill|Isobe Relay Station|ja|磯部中継局|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Isobe Relay Station|1=Isobe Relay Station|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11588064}}. The haiku pen name of {{ill|Seihō Shimada|ja|嶋田青峰|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seihō Shimada|1=Seihō Shimada|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11477392}}, a poet from nearby Matoya, is derived from this mountain.<ref>Hosoi (1996): p.48</ref> On May 25, 2016, Canadian Prime Minister {{ill|Justin Trudeau|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Justin Trudeau|qq=}} and his wife {{ill|Sophie Grégoire Trudeau|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sophie Grégoire Trudeau|qq=}} hiked Mount Aonomine during their visit for the {{ill|42nd G7 Summit|en|第42回先進国首脳会議|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/42nd G7 Summit|qq=}} in Ise-Shima.<ref>"Canadian Prime Minister Enjoys Hiking with His Wife on Their Wedding Anniversary," *Chunichi Shimbun*, May 26, 2016</ref> == Geography and Geology == Mount Aonomine is classified as part of the "Eastern Terrace District," a 100–115 meter hilly region within Toba City.<ref>Toba City History Compilation Office (1991): Upper Volume, p.18</ref> It serves as the source of the {{ill|Kamo River (Mie Prefecture)|ja|加茂川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamo River (Mie Prefecture)|1=加茂川|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q16279097}}, a secondary river.<ref>Toba City History Compilation Office (1991): Upper Volume, p.20</ref> Geologically, it belongs to the {{ill|Matoya Formation|ja|的矢層群|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Matoya Formation|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, which is part of the {{ill|Shimanto Belt|ja|四万十層群|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shimanto Belt|1=Shimanto group|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11254492}}, formed during the [[:en:Mesozoic|Mesozoic Era]].<ref>Kawai (1967): p.44</ref> == Aonomine Trails == The mountain has several trails collectively known as "Aonomine Trails": * '''Matsuo Trail''': Two routes (old and new) starting from Matsuo in Toba City. * '''Kenko Trail''': A route starting in Kenko in Toba City. * '''Isobe Trail''': Starting from Izawa-no-Miya Shrine in Shima City. * '''Matoya Trail''': A drivable route from Matoya Shrine in Shima City. * '''Gochi Trail''': A pedestrian-only route starting near [[:en:Gochi Station|Gochi Station]] in Shima City. == References == {{reflist|qq=}} == Further Reading == * Isobe Town History Compilation Committee, *Isobe Town History: Upper Volume*, 1997. * Kawai Masatora, "Exploring the Origins of the Japanese Archipelago," *Geological News*, 1967. * Toba City History Compilation Office, *Toba City History: Upper and Lower Volumes*, 1991. * Hosoi Keiji, "Seihō Shimada and the Haiku Literary Movement," *Haiku Literature Quarterly*, 1996. * Mie Prefecture Board of Education, *Historical Pathways of Ise: Asama-Toba Road and Others*, 1986. == Related Articles == * [[:en:Ise-Shima National Park|Ise-Shima National Park]] * [[:en:Sea lane|Navigation]] * [[:en:Sea mark|Landmarks]] * [[:en:Hiking|Hiking]] * {{ill|Hiyoriyama (Toba City)|ja|日和山 (鳥羽市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hiyoriyama (Toba City)|1=Mount Hiyori|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11505197}} == External Links == * [http://maikoh.net/corp/event/aominezan/ Shofuku-ji Temple - Aonomine Mountain Official Page]
{{translated page|ja|青峰山|version=92556814|comment=Imported full ja history}}
